evidence_quotes.quote: Verification may include legal name, address, phone, and potentially government ID, plus D-U-N-S numbers run by Dun & Bradstreet (US).
This raises GDPR and sovereignty concerns (data minimization, purpose limitation, transfers).
If identity checks are retained, mandate vendor-neutral verification (multiple providers, recognition of EU trust services under eIDAS) and due-process/appeals before revocation.

paragraphs.text: Google will require identity verification for any Android app to install on certified deviceseven when users sideload APKs or use third-party stores. Google argues sideloaded apps contain 50× more malware, but the new system does not check app binaries; it merely verifies the developers identity. This centralizes approval power for all distribution channels in the hands of one gatekeeper. Conflict with EU objectives. The DMA (Art. 6(4)) guarantees the installation and effective use of third-party apps/stores, allowing only strictly necessary and proportionate integrity safeguards. Making Googles private identity program a hard precondition for every install path is neither necessary nor proportionate when less-restrictive controls exist (Play Protect scanning, clear risk warnings, optional notarization, diversified trust providers). It risks converting Android into a walled garden while preserving a veneer of technical sideloading. Harms to EU consumers & SMEs: Competition: Alternative app stores must rely on Googles verification, shrinking their independence and contestability. F-Droid maintainers already flag this risk. Innovation/FOSS: Student and open-source maintainers are discouraged by new KYC hurdles and exposure of personal data. Privacy & sovereignty: Verification may include legal name, address, phone, and potentially government ID, plus D-U-N-S numbers run by Dun & Bradstreet (US). This raises GDPR and sovereignty concerns (data minimization, purpose limitation, transfers). Limited security efficacy: Identity checks dont vet app code; sophisticated actors can still verify identities. Meanwhile, legitimate EU developers shoulder costs/delays. Even coverage in the tech press notes Google wont review sideloaded app content. Recommendations for the DFA: Ban hard blocks on lawful installs based solely on gatekeeper-run developer identity; require choice-preserving protections (warnings, scanning). If identity checks are retained, mandate vendor-neutral verification (multiple providers, recognition of EU trust services under eIDAS) and due-process/appeals before revocation. Enforce data-minimization (no unnecessary documents; strict retention; EU data location by default). Guarantee low-friction routes for SMEs/FOSS/students (free verification, pseudonymous public profiles, or trusted EU intermediaries). Monitor compliance with DMA Art. 6(4) so sideloading and third-party stores remain effective in practice, not merely theoretical.
